businessOverview: Microsoft is the world's largest software company and one of the two leading hyperscale cloud and AI platform providers (alongside Amazon, with Google a close third). Founded in 1975 and headquartered in Redmond, WA, it operates across three reportable segments: Productivity and Business Processes (Microsoft 365, Office, Teams, Dynamics 365, LinkedIn), Intelligent Cloud (Azure, server products, GitHub, Nuance, enterprise services), and More Personal Computing (Windows, Surface devices, search/news advertising, and Xbox/gaming after the Activision Blizzard acquisition). The company has repositioned itself as an AI-first platform: it is OpenAI's largest commercial partner and Azure host, embeds 'Copilot' AI assistants across its product suite, and as of FY26 is scaling its own first-party MAI foundation models. In FQ3 2026 (quarter ended March 31, 2026) Microsoft reported $82.9B in total revenue, up 18% year over year, with Microsoft Cloud crossing $50B in a quarter for the first time at $54.5B.
productModel: Microsoft monetizes through three engines: (1) recurring software subscriptions — Microsoft 365 commercial/consumer seats, Dynamics, LinkedIn, and increasingly per-seat AI add-ons (Microsoft 365 Copilot, GitHub Copilot); (2) consumption-based cloud — Azure compute, storage, AI inference/training, and data services billed on usage, now the company's primary growth driver; and (3) transactional/device and advertising revenue — Windows OEM licensing, Surface, search & news ads, and Xbox content/services/hardware. The AI layer is sold both as premium per-seat SKUs (Copilot at ~$30/user/month) and as metered Azure AI/OpenAI API consumption. Management is signaling a shift toward more consumption-based pricing for AI products over time. Gross margins are anchored by high-margin software, partially offset by lower-margin Azure AI infrastructure and rising depreciation from the capex buildout.
marketContext: Microsoft sits at the center of the generative-AI platform shift. Azure is taking share in cloud infrastructure on the back of AI workloads, with Azure growth reaccelerating to 40% in FQ3 2026 after several quarters of deceleration, and an AI revenue run-rate surpassing $37B (up ~123% YoY). The defining tension is capital intensity: Microsoft guided to roughly $190B of capex for calendar 2026 — well above consensus — driven both by capacity expansion and by sharp memory/component price inflation, raising bear-case concerns about future depreciation drag and operating leverage if AI revenue can't keep pace. Strategically, the April 2026 restructuring of the OpenAI agreement removed Azure exclusivity and IP revenue-share obligations while extending IP licensing to 2032, and Microsoft's MAI Superintelligence Team (formed Nov 2025 under Mustafa Suleyman) launched seven in-house models at Build 2026, signaling a push toward AI self-sufficiency and a less OpenAI-dependent cost structure.
competitors: Google / Alphabet (Google Cloud, Workspace, Gemini AI)
OpenAI (increasingly a frontier-model competitor as well as partner)
Anthropic (frontier models, now also an Azure-hosted partner)
customers: Global enterprises and governments (Azure + M365 commercial)
OpenAI and other AI labs (Azure compute customers)

leadership.name: Satya Nadella
leadership.title: Chairman and Chief Executive Officer
leadership.background: Joined Microsoft in 1992; previously led the Server & Tools / Cloud and Enterprise group before becoming CEO. Architect of Microsoft's cloud-first, AI-first transformation.

leadership.name: Amy Hood
leadership.title: Executive Vice President and Chief Financial Officer
leadership.background: Joined Microsoft in 2002; held finance roles across the Business Division before becoming CFO. Credited with imposing operational and capital discipline through the cloud era.

leadership.name: Mustafa Suleyman
leadership.title: EVP and CEO of Microsoft AI; lead of the MAI Superintelligence Team
leadership.since: Joined Microsoft March 2024; Superintelligence Team formed Nov 2025
leadership.background: Co-founder of DeepMind and of Inflection AI; leads Microsoft's consumer AI (Copilot) and the in-house MAI model effort unveiled at Build 2026.

leadership.name: Brad Smith
leadership.title: Vice Chair and President
leadership.background: Microsoft's chief legal, policy, and government-affairs leader; long-tenured executive overseeing regulatory, AI policy, and corporate responsibility.

segments.name: Productivity and Business Processes
segments.note: Microsoft 365 commercial & consumer, Office, Teams, Dynamics 365, and LinkedIn. M365 Copilot per-seat AI is the key incremental driver (20M+ paid seats).

segments.name: Intelligent Cloud
segments.note: Azure (the primary growth engine, +40% YoY / +39% cc), server products, GitHub, Nuance, and enterprise services. AI revenue run-rate surpassed $37B (+~123% YoY).

segments.name: More Personal Computing
segments.note: Windows OEM, Surface devices, search & news advertising (Bing/Copilot), and Xbox/gaming. The flat-to-down legacy segment versus surging cloud.
